title: Digital Addiction
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Digital Addiction
summary: Scripture's warnings about being mastered by anything, its call to guard the mind, and its emphasis on purposeful living speak directly to the modern struggle with compulsive screen use.
body:
Paul articulates the principle that governs digital addiction: all things are lawful for me, but I will not be mastered by anything [verse:1cor.6.12]. Smartphones and screens are not sinful, but when they control behavior rather than serving it — when the first and last act of every day is checking a device — the dynamic has shifted from use to bondage.

The mind is the battleground. Paul commands believers to take every thought captive [verse:2cor.10.5]. Digital platforms are designed to capture attention through algorithmic engagement — infinite scroll, notifications, autoplay. Intentional resistance to this is not paranoia; it is the spiritual discipline of guarding the mind.

Jesus teaches that where your treasure is, there your heart will be also [verse:matt.6.21]. Attention is a form of treasure. What we give our sustained focus to shapes what we love, and what we love determines who we become. The psalmist models the alternative: I will set no worthless thing before my eyes [verse:ps.101.3].

The call to sabbath rest carries fresh relevance. God built rhythms of work and rest into creation [verse:gen.2.2-3]. Constant digital connectivity eliminates the boundaries between engagement and rest. Deliberate disconnection is not asceticism — it is obedience to the rhythm God designed.

Freedom from digital compulsion follows the same path as freedom from any bondage: walking by the Spirit rather than gratifying the desires of the flesh [verse:gal.5.16].

verses.annotation: Paul's refusal to be mastered by anything — the foundational principle for any form of addiction.
Taking every thought captive — active resistance to patterns that hijack attention.
Where your treasure is, your heart follows — attention as a form of devotion.
Setting nothing worthless before the eyes — choosing what deserves sustained focus.
God rests on the seventh day — sabbath as a designed rhythm, not an optional upgrade.
Walking by the Spirit as the daily counter to compulsive gratification.
Filling the mind with what is true, noble, pure — the opposite of algorithmic content consumption.

scholars.quote: Fee argues that Paul's 'not be mastered' language in 1 Corinthians 6 establishes a universal principle: any good thing that becomes a controlling force has crossed the line from liberty to bondage.
scholars.source: The First Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, 2014)

scholars.quote: Harris reads 2 Corinthians 10:5 as Paul calling for active cognitive discipline — not passive resignation to whatever thoughts intrude but intentional alignment of the mind with truth.
scholars.source: The Second Epistle to the Corinthians (Harris, 2005)

scholars.quote: Keener notes that Jesus' teaching about treasure and heart in Matthew 6 reveals a directional relationship: attention shapes affection, which means what we give our eyes and time to is a spiritual decision.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)
